summary: Stancil argues that most SQL chatbots are aiming at the wrong problem: LLMs can draft queries in toy schemas, but real-world analysis requires extensive context, governance, and BI-like infrastructure. He contends that LLMs are better at creative, hypothesis-generating work than at precise, deductive querying, which flips the traditional promise that tech automates the tedious parts so humans can do the fun parts.
money_quotes: For bots to be successful query writers—and even harder, for them to be proper analysts that can answer questions about a business—LLMs will likely only be a small part of the solution.
Put differently, the LLM isn’t an application, or even close to one.
More bluntly, LLMs and query writing are fundamentally mismatched.
Because so far, one of the most striking things about LLMs is that they’re much better at the creative parts of analysis than they are at the mechanical parts.
In other words, it inverted the workflow between human and computer.
key_insight: LLMs are ill-suited to reliable query generation at scale but excel at creative analytical reasoning, so chatbots risk automating the fun parts while leaving humans with the drudgery.
